APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work addresses a core NLP problem (automatic synset induction) with a novel, well-motivated graph-based meta-clustering method (WATSET) and evaluates it against multiple strong baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) on four gold standards (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN) using standard metrics (precision/recall/F-score). The methodology (sense-aware disambiguation, sense graph construction, hard clustering) and reported hyperparameters match expectations for a top-tier submission, and the paper situates itself with thorough related work coverage and multilingual experiments (English/Russian). From a venue-fit perspective—audience, problem formulation, methods and evaluation standards—this is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML conference. | B(5): This paper addresses an important NLP problem (automatic synset induction) that is squarely within the remit of top-tier NLP/ML venues, proposing a novel graph-based meta-clustering method (WATSET) and integrating word embeddings and word sense induction. The methodology and empirical evaluation meet community standards: comparisons to five established baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM), evaluation on multiple gold resources (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN) using precision/recall/F-score, and discussion of limitations (dictionary sparsity, dataset domain differences). The presentation and coverage of related work are thorough and the task and audience are appropriate for this venue, with clear potential impact for resource construction and downstream NLP applications.

reviewer_a.justification: This work addresses a core NLP problem (automatic synset induction) with a novel, well-motivated graph-based meta-clustering method (WATSET) and evaluates it against multiple strong baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) on four gold standards (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN) using standard metrics (precision/recall/F-score). The methodology (sense-aware disambiguation, sense graph construction, hard clustering) and reported hyperparameters match expectations for a top-tier submission, and the paper situates itself with thorough related work coverage and multilingual experiments (English/Russian). From a venue-fit perspective—audience, problem formulation, methods and evaluation standards—this is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important NLP problem (automatic synset induction) that is squarely within the remit of top-tier NLP/ML venues, proposing a novel graph-based meta-clustering method (WATSET) and integrating word embeddings and word sense induction. The methodology and empirical evaluation meet community standards: comparisons to five established baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM), evaluation on multiple gold resources (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN) using precision/recall/F-score, and discussion of limitations (dictionary sparsity, dataset domain differences). The presentation and coverage of related work are thorough and the task and audience are appropriate for this venue, with clear potential impact for resource construction and downstream NLP applications.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experimental evaluation is strong in breadth—comparison to five competitive baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) across four gold datasets (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN) with precision/recall/F-score and reported hyperparameters—supporting the main performance claims. However, the paper appears to lack systematic ablation and sensitivity analyses (e.g., robustness to graph construction choices, embedding types, and clustering hyperparameters) and does not report runtime/complexity or provide code availability for reproducibility. Related work coverage is thorough and limitations (dependency on input synonymy sparsity and gold-standard variability) are discussed, but more empirical failure-case analysis and reproducibility details are needed to elevate methodological confidence to the highest level. | B(4): The experimental evaluation is strong in breadth: comparisons to five state-of-the-art graph clustering baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) across four gold standards (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN) support the main claims that WATSET improves F-score for synset induction. The paper reports hyperparameters and discusses real limitations (input-synonymy sparsity causing recall loss) and situates itself well among prior graph-based WSI work (e.g., UoY and hierarchical graph methods). However, I find limited evidence of systematic ablation or sensitivity analysis (e.g., effect of embedding choices, sense induction parameters, or disambiguation errors) and the absence of mentioned released code reduces reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters. Overall the work is thorough with minor gaps in analysis and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is strong in breadth—comparison to five competitive baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) across four gold datasets (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN) with precision/recall/F-score and reported hyperparameters—supporting the main performance claims. However, the paper appears to lack systematic ablation and sensitivity analyses (e.g., robustness to graph construction choices, embedding types, and clustering hyperparameters) and does not report runtime/complexity or provide code availability for reproducibility. Related work coverage is thorough and limitations (dependency on input synonymy sparsity and gold-standard variability) are discussed, but more empirical failure-case analysis and reproducibility details are needed to elevate methodological confidence to the highest level.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is strong in breadth: comparisons to five state-of-the-art graph clustering baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) across four gold standards (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN) support the main claims that WATSET improves F-score for synset induction. The paper reports hyperparameters and discusses real limitations (input-synonymy sparsity causing recall loss) and situates itself well among prior graph-based WSI work (e.g., UoY and hierarchical graph methods). However, I find limited evidence of systematic ablation or sensitivity analysis (e.g., effect of embedding choices, sense induction parameters, or disambiguation errors) and the absence of mentioned released code reduces reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters. Overall the work is thorough with minor gaps in analysis and reproducibility.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's primary claims—that WATSET induces synsets via sense-aware graph disambiguation and outperforms five graph clustering baselines on four gold standards—are supported by empirical results across English and Russian datasets and reported hyperparameters. However, methodological gaps remain: I see no mention of code release, limited discussion of statistical significance or runtime/complexity trade-offs, and no reported robustness or ablation studies (e.g., sensitivity to graph construction, embedding choices, or clustering hyperparameters). These are minor but important omissions that do not invalidate the results, so the work is technically sound overall but would benefit from additional experimental validation and reproducibility artifacts. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: WATSET is evaluated against five established graph clustering baselines on four gold standards (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN) and shows superior F-score. There are no formal mathematical proofs required for the proposed meta-clustering algorithm, and the methodological pipeline (ego-network WSI, neighbor disambiguation, hard clustering) is coherent and appropriate; however the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release, which are minor omissions for reproducibility and confidence in reported gains. The authors acknowledge key limitations (input dictionary sparsity) and the conclusions about improved synset induction are consistent with the presented experimental results.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's primary claims—that WATSET induces synsets via sense-aware graph disambiguation and outperforms five graph clustering baselines on four gold standards—are supported by empirical results across English and Russian datasets and reported hyperparameters. However, methodological gaps remain: I see no mention of code release, limited discussion of statistical significance or runtime/complexity trade-offs, and no reported robustness or ablation studies (e.g., sensitivity to graph construction, embedding choices, or clustering hyperparameters). These are minor but important omissions that do not invalidate the results, so the work is technically sound overall but would benefit from additional experimental validation and reproducibility artifacts.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: WATSET is evaluated against five established graph clustering baselines on four gold standards (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN) and shows superior F-score. There are no formal mathematical proofs required for the proposed meta-clustering algorithm, and the methodological pipeline (ego-network WSI, neighbor disambiguation, hard clustering) is coherent and appropriate; however the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release, which are minor omissions for reproducibility and confidence in reported gains. The authors acknowledge key limitations (input dictionary sparsity) and the conclusions about improved synset induction are consistent with the presented experimental results.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from problem motivation through method and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (WATSET meta-clustering and its two-stage sense-aware graph construction). Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and well-defined (e.g., graph construction, sense induction, disambiguation, clustering) and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is described as good, though minor presentation improvements could make some technical steps (e.g., edge-case handling and graph sparsity effects) even clearer. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from problem motivation through the WATSET method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (novel meta-clustering for synset induction using sense-aware graphs). Notation and methodology are described consistently, and figures/tables/examples appear effective and informative given the reported experiments on WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, and YARN against multiple baselines. Minor presentation issues include limited mention of code availability and some dependence on dataset variation that affects cross-dataset comparability, but overall the writing is clear and largely free of ambiguity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from problem motivation through method and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (WATSET meta-clustering and its two-stage sense-aware graph construction). Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and well-defined (e.g., graph construction, sense induction, disambiguation, clustering) and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is described as good, though minor presentation improvements could make some technical steps (e.g., edge-case handling and graph sparsity effects) even clearer.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from problem motivation through the WATSET method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (novel meta-clustering for synset induction using sense-aware graphs). Notation and methodology are described consistently, and figures/tables/examples appear effective and informative given the reported experiments on WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, and YARN against multiple baselines. Minor presentation issues include limited mention of code availability and some dependence on dataset variation that affects cross-dataset comparability, but overall the writing is clear and largely free of ambiguity.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—automatic synset induction—and introduces WATSET, a novel meta-clustering approach that disambiguates ego-networks and then performs sense-aware clustering. Empirically it outperforms five established graph clustering baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) across four gold standards for English and Russian, which suggests a strong methodological advance likely to influence researchers building lexical resources. The cross-lingual evaluation and explicit handling of ambiguity open useful directions for follow-up work (e.g., robustness to graph sparsity and ablation of sense induction components). Impact is tempered somewhat by dependence on input dictionary completeness and the absence of mentioned code/release, so I judge the likely influence as strong but not transformative. | B(4): The paper tackles an important, practical problem—automatic synset induction—by proposing WATSET, a novel meta-clustering pipeline that combines sense-aware disambiguation with hard clustering and shows consistent F-score gains over five established baselines on four gold standards (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN). Its approach—disambiguating ego-networks and building a sense-aware graph—is likely to change how researchers construct fuzzy synsets from ambiguous synonym graphs and is immediately applicable to resource-poor languages where dictionary coverage is sparse. While not a paradigm shift, the method opens clear follow-up directions (better handling of missing edges, integration with embeddings, downstream evaluation) and is likely to be built upon by both lexical resource creators and downstream NLP practitioners.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—automatic synset induction—and introduces WATSET, a novel meta-clustering approach that disambiguates ego-networks and then performs sense-aware clustering. Empirically it outperforms five established graph clustering baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) across four gold standards for English and Russian, which suggests a strong methodological advance likely to influence researchers building lexical resources. The cross-lingual evaluation and explicit handling of ambiguity open useful directions for follow-up work (e.g., robustness to graph sparsity and ablation of sense induction components). Impact is tempered somewhat by dependence on input dictionary completeness and the absence of mentioned code/release, so I judge the likely influence as strong but not transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important, practical problem—automatic synset induction—by proposing WATSET, a novel meta-clustering pipeline that combines sense-aware disambiguation with hard clustering and shows consistent F-score gains over five established baselines on four gold standards (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN). Its approach—disambiguating ego-networks and building a sense-aware graph—is likely to change how researchers construct fuzzy synsets from ambiguous synonym graphs and is immediately applicable to resource-poor languages where dictionary coverage is sparse. While not a paradigm shift, the method opens clear follow-up directions (better handling of missing edges, integration with embeddings, downstream evaluation) and is likely to be built upon by both lexical resource creators and downstream NLP practitioners.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a practical meta-clustering pipeline (WATSET) that combines per-node ego-network word sense induction, neighbor disambiguation, and global hard clustering to produce fuzzy synsets. While this particular combination and the application to synset induction appear usefully engineered, the core ideas—graph-based WSI, disambiguation via local clustering, and global clustering of a sense-aware graph—closely follow prior graph-based WSI work (e.g., UoY graphs, hierarchical/random-graph WSI). The claimed specific technical tweaks (e.g., weighting without a corpus, removing high-triangle nodes) provide modest novelty but are essentially non-trivial extensions rather than fundamentally new methodology, so the contribution is an incremental yet notable advance rather than a breakthrough. | B(3): The paper presents a sensible and practically useful combination of existing graph-based word sense induction and clustering techniques (ego-network WSI, neighbor disambiguation, then global hard clustering) and adds the meta-clustering perspective (WATSET) to produce fuzzy synsets. However, core ideas—graph-based WSI/disambiguation and clustering for sense induction—are well-established (e.g., UoY graphs for WSI, hierarchical/random-graph WSI approaches, collocation-graph methods), so the contribution reads as a non-trivial but incremental integration and engineering advance rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The reported novelties (optional corpus-free weighting, triangle-node removal observation) are useful but limited, so an expert would find the method informative but not surprising.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a practical meta-clustering pipeline (WATSET) that combines per-node ego-network word sense induction, neighbor disambiguation, and global hard clustering to produce fuzzy synsets. While this particular combination and the application to synset induction appear usefully engineered, the core ideas—graph-based WSI, disambiguation via local clustering, and global clustering of a sense-aware graph—closely follow prior graph-based WSI work (e.g., UoY graphs, hierarchical/random-graph WSI). The claimed specific technical tweaks (e.g., weighting without a corpus, removing high-triangle nodes) provide modest novelty but are essentially non-trivial extensions rather than fundamentally new methodology, so the contribution is an incremental yet notable advance rather than a breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a sensible and practically useful combination of existing graph-based word sense induction and clustering techniques (ego-network WSI, neighbor disambiguation, then global hard clustering) and adds the meta-clustering perspective (WATSET) to produce fuzzy synsets. However, core ideas—graph-based WSI/disambiguation and clustering for sense induction—are well-established (e.g., UoY graphs for WSI, hierarchical/random-graph WSI approaches, collocation-graph methods), so the contribution reads as a non-trivial but incremental integration and engineering advance rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The reported novelties (optional corpus-free weighting, triangle-node removal observation) are useful but limited, so an expert would find the method informative but not surprising.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares WATSET to a reasonable set of strong, relevant graph-clustering baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) across multiple gold standards with common evaluation metrics (precision/recall/F-score), and reports hyperparameters — supporting fair empirical comparison. The methodology differences are described and related work coverage is said to be thorough, including prior graph-based WSI approaches. Minor gaps remain: the baselines are largely classical graph-clustering methods (few very recent neural or embedding-first synset induction approaches are considered), and it is not explicit whether the authors acknowledge cases where prior methods match WATSET or provide statistical significance/robustness analyses. Overall the comparisons are solid but not fully exhaustive. | B(4): The paper compares against a solid set of state-of-the-art graph clustering baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) on multiple gold standards (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN) using standard metrics (precision/recall/F-score) and reports hyperparameters, which supports fair experimental conditions. The authors also situate their work within graph-based WSI literature (e.g., UoY 2010 and other graph WSI methods) and note limitations affecting recall, indicating awareness of prior methods’ strengths. Minor omissions remain: some classic WSI approaches based on hierarchical/random-graph models or alternative induction paradigms could have been included in empirical comparisons to fully establish superiority across methodological families. Overall the comparisons are thorough and fair, but not exhaustively inclusive of all relevant WSI alternatives.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares WATSET to a reasonable set of strong, relevant graph-clustering baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) across multiple gold standards with common evaluation metrics (precision/recall/F-score), and reports hyperparameters — supporting fair empirical comparison. The methodology differences are described and related work coverage is said to be thorough, including prior graph-based WSI approaches. Minor gaps remain: the baselines are largely classical graph-clustering methods (few very recent neural or embedding-first synset induction approaches are considered), and it is not explicit whether the authors acknowledge cases where prior methods match WATSET or provide statistical significance/robustness analyses. Overall the comparisons are solid but not fully exhaustive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a solid set of state-of-the-art graph clustering baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) on multiple gold standards (WordNet, BabelNet, RuThes, YARN) using standard metrics (precision/recall/F-score) and reports hyperparameters, which supports fair experimental conditions. The authors also situate their work within graph-based WSI literature (e.g., UoY 2010 and other graph WSI methods) and note limitations affecting recall, indicating awareness of prior methods’ strengths. Minor omissions remain: some classic WSI approaches based on hierarchical/random-graph models or alternative induction paradigms could have been included in empirical comparisons to fully establish superiority across methodological families. Overall the comparisons are thorough and fair, but not exhaustively inclusive of all relevant WSI alternatives.

main_claims: Proposes WATSET, a novel graph-based meta-clustering method to automatically induce synsets from an ambiguous synonymy graph using word embeddings and word sense induction.
WATSET disambiguates word neighbors to build a sense-aware graph and then performs global hard clustering to produce synsets (meta-clustering enabling fuzzy clustering).
The method outperforms five state-of-the-art graph clustering methods (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) in terms of F-score on four gold standard datasets for English and Russian.
_summary.methodology: Build a weighted synonymy graph from dictionaries and word embeddings; perform graph-based word sense induction on ego-networks to split nodes into senses; disambiguate neighbor words to create a sense-aware graph; apply hard clustering on the disambiguated graph to induce synsets.
limitations: Dependence on completeness/sparseness of the input synonymy dictionary; missing bridges in the input graph can lead to smaller-than-desired synsets (lower recall).
Variation in gold standards (domain-specific vs. generic) causes differences in absolute evaluation scores and limits cross-dataset comparability.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - automatic synset induction / lexical resource construction

reviewer_a.name: Methodology Specialist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML/NLP engineer with a PhD in computational linguistics and 8+ years building and evaluating graph-based NLP systems and lexical resources.
traits: focuses on experimental design, reproducibility, and clarity of implementation details (hyperparameters, datasets, code availability)
requires strong baselines, proper statistical evaluation, and ablation studies to support claims about method components
looks for robustness analyses (sensitivity to graph construction, embeddings, and clustering hyperparameters) and clear error analysis
values precise descriptions of algorithmic complexity, runtime, and resource requirements
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper primarily on methodological rigor, empirical validity, and whether the experimental setup substantiates the main claims.

reviewer_b.name: Domain & Impact Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher with a track record in lexical semantics, word sense induction, and lexical resource construction; experienced in integrating academic methods into lexicography and downstream NLP applications.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior work in synset induction and word sense induction, noting conceptual advances and overlaps
values broader significance: applicability to resource-poor languages, impact on downstream tasks, and practical usability of induced synsets
checks whether evaluation datasets and metrics reflect real-world needs and whether improvements are meaningful beyond small numeric gains
interprets results in the context of existing literature and suggests next steps for adoption or extension
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges the paper by how it advances the field, its originality, and the practical importance and limitations of the proposed approach.
